title: Workforce and Organisational Capacity
content:
What recent experience shows

Across recent reorganisations, workforce change has been one of the most significant and destabilising features of transition. Reductions have typically focused on management, corporate and support functions, but the impact has extended well beyond those areas.

Business cases often frame workforce change as a technical exercise, centred on the removal of duplicated posts. In practice, councils report a more complex pattern of voluntary redundancy, early retirement, restructuring and staff departure driven by prolonged uncertainty. Middle management layers have been particularly affected, reducing organisational resilience at a point when demand on remaining staff increases.

Councils commonly report loss of institutional knowledge, emerging skills gaps in critical services such as planning and adult social care, increased workload pressure on remaining staff, and difficulties recruiting into new structures while future arrangements remain unclear. These impacts have direct consequences for service performance and delivery confidence.

What this tells us

Workforce change during reorganisation is not neutral or self-contained. It represents a sustained organisational shock that affects capacity, morale and delivery capability. Where uncertainty is prolonged, councils experience higher attrition and weaker retention of key skills, often undermining the efficiency and service improvements that reorganisation is intended to deliver.

What this means for future reorganisations

Based on recent experience, future reorganisations should treat workforce capacity as a core delivery risk. Early clarity on structures and roles, sustained staff engagement, and explicit protection of frontline and technical expertise are essential. Workforce plans that focus narrowly on headcount reduction risk weakening organisational performance at the very moment stability is most needed.
